perturbation.original_text: The Association, in accordance with and pursuant to its plan of conversion adopted by the Board of Directors of the Association (the "Plan"), intends to be converted from a federally-chartered mutual savings and loan association to a federally-chartered stock savings bank and will sell all of its issued and outstanding stock to the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: The Association, in accordance with and pursuant to its plan of conversion adopted by the Board of Directors of the Association (the "Plan"), intends to be converted from a federally-chartered mutual savings and loan association to an equivalent state-chartered stock savings bank and will sell all of its issued and outstanding stock to the Company.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "federally-chartered" to "equivalent state-chartered" creates ambiguity and potential conflict.  The original text aligns with the standard conversion process regulated at the federal level. The modification suggests a state charter, which would be governed by different regulations, thus contradicting the assumption of federal oversight implicit in the rest of the agreement and potentially jeopardizing regulatory compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: This law defines the regulations under which a federally chartered savings association operates. Changing it to 'state-chartered' implies compliance with state laws, fundamentally altering the regulatory framework and contradicting the established federal structure presumed in the original contract, specifically the oversight by the OTS mentioned later.

Federal savings associations (a) In general In order to provide thrift institutions for the deposit of funds and for the extension of credit for homes and other goods and services, the Comptroller of the Currency is authorized, under such regulations as the Comptroller of the Currency may prescribe- (1) to provide for the organization, incorporation, examination, operation, and regulation of associations to be known as Fe
perturbation.justification: The change from 'federally-chartered' to 'state-chartered' directly contradicts the regulatory framework established in 12 USC 1464, which governs federal savings associations. By implying compliance with state laws, the modified text undermines the federal oversight and regulations mandated by this statute. The original text adheres to federal guidelines, while the changed text introduces a conflicting and potentially non-compliant standard.

perturbation.original_text: the deposit accounts of the Association are insured by the FDIC up to the applicable limits. Upon consummation of the Conversion, the liquidation account for the benefit of Eligible Account Holders and Supplemental Eligible Account Holders will be duly established in accordance with the requirements of the Conversion Regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: the deposit accounts of the Association are insured at the discretion of the Association's board of directors. Upon consummation of the Conversion, the liquidation account for the benefit of Eligible Account Holders and Supplemental Eligible Account Holders will be optional.
perturbation.explanation: This modification directly contradicts the mandatory nature of FDIC insurance and the establishment of liquidation accounts. It makes both discretionary, which is not legally permissible for federally chartered savings associations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 12 U.S. Code § 1811 - Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation
perturbation.law_explanation: This law establishes the FDIC and mandates deposit insurance for eligible institutions. Making deposit insurance discretionary violates this core requirement. Similarly, Conversion Regulations outline how liquidation accounts must be established, and making them 'optional' directly goes against those regulations.

Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (a) Establishment of Corporation There is hereby established a Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (hereinafter referred to as the "Corporation") which shall insure, as hereinafter provided, the deposits of all banks and savings associations which are entitled to the benefits of insurance under this chapter, and which shall have the powers hereinafter
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts federal law regarding deposit insurance and liquidation accounts. The original text states that deposit accounts 'are insured by the FDIC,' while the changed text makes this insurance 'at the discretion of the Association's board of directors.' Similarly, the original text says the liquidation account 'will be duly established,' while the changed text makes it 'optional.' According to 12 USC 1811, a Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation is established to insure deposits of banks and savings associations. The changed text's introduction of discretion directly opposes the mandatory nature of FDIC insurance as stated in the original text and supported by the law snippet. This constitutes a direct contradiction by changing a mandatory requirement to an optional one.

perturbation.original_text: All subscription funds received by Capital Resources (and if by check shall be made payable to the Company) or by other NASD registered broker-dealers soliciting subscriptions (if any) shall be transmitted (either by U.S. Mail or similar type of transmittal) to the Company by noon of the following business day.
perturbation.changed_text: All subscription funds received by Capital Resources (and if by check shall be made payable to Capital Resources) or by other NASD registered broker-dealers soliciting subscriptions (if any) may be transmitted to the Company at the discretion of Capital Resources.
perturbation.explanation: This changes the recipient of the check and eliminates the explicit requirement for prompt transmittal of subscription funds to the company.  Altering the payee to Capital Resources and removing the strict timeline introduces ambiguity and potential conflict. Rule 15c2-4 under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 mandates specific procedures for handling customer funds in securities offerings.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 17 CFR § 240.15c2-4 - Transmission or Use of Customer Funds or Securities
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 15c2-4 outlines specific requirements for broker-dealers handling customer funds in contingent offerings. Allowing Capital Resources to receive checks payable to themselves and delaying transmission directly violates the safeguarding provisions intended to protect investors' funds.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.15c2-4 - Transmission or maintenance of payments received in connection with underwritings. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.15c2-4 - Transmission or maintenance of payments received in connection with underwritings. CFR prev | next § 240.15c2-4 Transmission or maintenance of payments received in connection with underwritings. It shall constitute a “fraudulent, deceptive, or manipulative act or practice” as used in section 15(c)(2) of the Act, for any broker, dealer or municipal securities dealer participating in any distribution of securities, other than a firm-commitment underwriting , to accept any part of the sale price of any security being distributed unless: (a) The money or other consideration received is promptly transmitted to the persons entitled thereto; or (b) If the distribution is being made on an “all-or-none” basis, or on any other basis which contemplates that payment is not to be made to the person on whose behalf the distribution is being made until some further event or contingency occurs, (1) the money or other consideration received is promptly deposited in a separate bank account, as agent or trustee for the persons who have the beneficial interests therein, until the appropriate event or contingency has occurred, and then the funds are promptly transmitted or returned to the persons entitled thereto, or (2) all such funds are promptly transmitted to a bank which has agreed in writing to hold all such funds in escrow for the persons who have the beneficial interests therein and to transmit or return such funds directly to the persons entitled thereto when the appropriate event or contingency has occurred. [ 41 FR 22826 , June 7, 1976] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The change contradicts Rule 15c2-4 under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. The original text mandates that subscription funds, especially checks, are promptly transmitted to the company. The modified text allows Capital Resources to receive checks payable to themselves and gives them discretion over when to transmit the funds. This directly violates the safeguarding provisions, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2, specifically 17 CFR § 240.15c2-4, states that it is a "fraudulent, deceptive, or manipulative act or practice" for a broker-dealer to accept funds without promptly transmitting them to the appropriate parties. The modification removes the explicit requirement for prompt transmittal and alters the payee, directly contradicting this rule.
